title: Tokenizer Training Workflow
concept: Train and lock the tokenizer first, verify vocabulary coverage across languages, then embed it in the LLM. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Distributed Pretraining.'

exercises.prompt: What is the correct order for setting up a tokenizer and an LLM?

options.text: Train and lock the tokenizer first, then train the LLM.
Train the LLM first, then build the tokenizer.
Train both at the same time on the same processor.

exercises.explain: The tokenizer must be trained, verified, and locked before we embed it in the LLM and start training.

cards.title: The Gatekeeper of Language
cards.body: Before an LLM can read a single sentence, it needs to learn its alphabet. But LLMs don't read letters—they read 'tokens'. How do we build this vocabulary, and why does it have to be fully completed and locked before the brain of the LLM even starts training? Let's find out!

cards.title: Tokenizer Training Workflow
cards.body: Tokenizer training is the process of scanning huge amounts of text to find the best subword units, building a vocabulary, and freezing it. We must ensure this vocabulary covers all intended languages before we hook it up to the LLM. Once training starts, this vocabulary cannot change!

cards.title: The Translator's Dictionary
cards.body: Think of a tokenizer like a specialized dictionary for a translator. If you are translating a book, you don't build the dictionary while you write. First, you read lots of books, write down all the common words and parts of words to build your dictionary, and bind it. Only once that dictionary is completely printed and locked do you hand it to the writer to begin drafting the actual book.

cards.title: Building a Multilingual Bridge
cards.body: Imagine you want to train a multilingual assistant. First, you run a training algorithm on English, Spanish, and French text. The tokenizer outputs a list of 50,000 subwords. You check to make sure French accents and Spanish symbols are properly represented. Finally, you lock this vocabulary file and plug it into your model as the input layer.

cards.title: Myth: Tokenizers Learn on the Fly
cards.body: Many people think tokenizers learn and update their vocabulary dynamically while the LLM is training. In reality, the tokenizer is trained and frozen beforehand. If you changed the tokenizer's vocabulary mid-training, all the numbers the LLM associated with words would suddenly mean something else, breaking the model!

cards.title: Workflow Stability
cards.body: A comparison of a correct, stable workflow versus an incorrect, unstable tokenizer workflow.

left.text: Tokenizer vocabulary is trained, verified for languages, and locked before LLM training begins.

right.text: Changing the tokenizer during training, which scrambles word meanings and ruins the LLM.

exercises.prompt: Why is it dangerous to modify a tokenizer's vocabulary after the LLM has started training?

options.text: It changes the meaning of the numbers the LLM has already learned.
It makes the computer run out of storage space instantly.
It converts all text to emojis.
It speeds up the training too much for the server to handle.

exercises.explain: Changing the tokenizer mid-training alters the meaning of the token IDs, which confuses the model and ruins the training progress.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The tokenizer training workflow must be completely finished and locked before we begin pretraining the LLM.
exercises.explain: The tokenizer must be fully trained, verified, and locked before the LLM itself begins pretraining.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing step in the tokenizer setup workflow:
exercises.sentence: First you collect text, then you train the vocabulary, next you check language coverage, and finally you freeze the tokenizer before starting the LLM training.
exercises.explain: We must freeze the tokenizer's vocabulary before LLM training starts so the numerical IDs assigned to words remain constant.

exercises.prompt: Which of these descriptions of a tokenizer workflow is incorrect and likely AI-generated?

options.text: First, train the tokenizer on your dataset, verify it supports your target languages, lock the vocabulary file, and load it into the model configuration.
Keep the tokenizer connected to a live database so it can automatically add new words and update its token IDs during the LLM's training epochs.
Build the tokenizer vocabulary independently, check for missing characters across languages, freeze the tokenizer, and then initialize the LLM's embedding layers.

exercises.explain: Option B is AI-generated because it suggests updating the vocabulary dynamically during neural network training, which is incorrect and would break the model.

exercises.prompt: You are building a brand new LLM from scratch. What is the correct sequence of steps to prepare the tokenizer?

options.text: Train the LLM first, then extract the tokenizer from the LLM's weights.
Train the tokenizer, lock it, check language coverage, and embed it before training the LLM.
Constantly update the tokenizer's vocabulary while the LLM is learning.
Let the tokenizer adapt dynamically to every new book the LLM reads.

exercises.explain: Locking the tokenizer protects the LLM from chaotic shifts in word definitions. Next up: Distributed Pretraining.
